rubric.specific_prediction: In complex, partially observable, multi-step text games, LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures (combining structured semantic representations such as knowledge/belief graphs with episodic/reflection-based memory) achieve higher task success rates and final scores than agents using only structured memory, only episodic memory, or naive in-context windowing alone.
operational_signals: Direct comparison of hybrid memory agents against single-modality baselines (structured-only, episodic-only, or context-window-only)
Task success rate, completion rate, score, or reward metrics in text game environments
Environments characterized as partially observable, multi-step, or compositional (e.g., TextWorld, Jericho, interactive fiction)
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report performance metrics (e.g., success rate, score, reward) for both a hybrid memory agent and at least one single-modality baseline (structured-only, episodic-only, or window-only) on the same text game task with partial observability and multi-step requirements.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents consistently outperform all single-modality baselines across multiple tasks or environments, especially on complex or long-horizon scenarios.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents (structured-only, episodic-only, or context-window-only) match or exceed hybrid memory agents' performance on complex, partially observable, multi-step text games.

rubric.specific_prediction: In complex text games, LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures reach target performance thresholds in fewer episodes, steps, or interactions than agents using only a single memory modality.
operational_signals: Learning curves showing performance vs. number of episodes, trials, or environment steps
Comparative sample efficiency metrics (episodes to convergence, steps to threshold performance)
Direct comparison between hybrid and single-modality memory agents
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report learning dynamics (e.g., performance over time/episodes) for both hybrid and single-modality memory agents, demonstrating convergence speed or sample efficiency differences.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents reach performance milestones significantly faster (fewer episodes or steps) than single-modality baselines.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents converge as fast as or faster than hybrid memory agents to equivalent performance levels.

rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures demonstrate better zero-shot or few-shot generalization to new text game environments, unseen tasks, or harder variants compared to agents using only structured memory, only episodic memory, or naive context windowing.
operational_signals: Transfer or generalization experiments (training on one set of games/tasks, testing on new or harder variants)
Performance metrics on held-out environments or difficulty levels
Explicit comparison of hybrid vs. single-modality agents on out-of-distribution tasks
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both hybrid and single-modality memory agents on at least one generalization scenario (new environment, unseen task type, or increased difficulty) distinct from training conditions.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents maintain higher performance or show smaller performance degradation on new/harder tasks compared to single-modality baselines.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents generalize as well as or better than hybrid memory agents to new environments or harder task variants.

rubric.specific_prediction: In long-horizon text games, hybrid memory agents achieve better or equivalent performance compared to full-history retention agents while using fewer tokens or reduced memory footprint.
operational_signals: Comparison of hybrid memory vs. full-history or unlimited context baselines
Performance metrics (success rate, score) alongside memory or token usage statistics
Long-horizon or multi-episode task scenarios
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare hybrid memory agents against full-history baselines on long-horizon tasks, reporting both performance and resource metrics (e.g., tokens, memory size, context length).
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents match or exceed full-history performance while demonstrating lower token usage, memory footprint, or computational cost.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history retention consistently outperforms hybrid memory agents on long-horizon tasks, or hybrid memory requires comparable or greater resource usage without performance gains.

rubric.specific_prediction: In text game tasks that require only short-term recall within the LLM's context window and have simple state/action structure, hybrid memory architectures show negligible performance improvement over naive in-context windowing or no memory augmentation.
operational_signals: Comparison of memory-augmented vs. baseline agents on explicitly short-horizon or simple tasks
Task descriptions indicating full observability, short episode length, or trivial state structure
Performance metrics showing small or no effect size
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate hybrid or augmented memory agents against minimal-memory baselines on tasks explicitly characterized as short-horizon, fully observable, or simple, reporting comparable performance metrics.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Performance differences between hybrid memory and baseline agents are minimal (within error margins or statistically insignificant) on short-horizon or simple tasks.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Hybrid memory provides substantial performance gains even on tasks requiring only short-term recall within the context window and having trivial state structure.

rubric.specific_prediction: Removing the structured memory component (e.g., knowledge graph, belief graph) from a hybrid memory agent results in degraded performance on complex text games, with the agent performing worse than the full hybrid system.
operational_signals: Ablation studies explicitly disabling or removing structured memory components
Performance comparison between full hybrid agent and structured-component-ablated variant
Complex or compositional task environments
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report an ablation experiment that disables the structured memory component of a hybrid agent and compares performance to the full hybrid system on the same task.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Removing structured memory leads to measurable performance degradation (lower success rate, score, or reward) compared to the full hybrid agent.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Ablating structured memory has no significant negative effect, or improves performance compared to the full hybrid system.

rubric.specific_prediction: Removing the episodic or reflection-based memory component from a hybrid memory agent results in degraded performance on complex text games, with the agent performing worse than the full hybrid system.
operational_signals: Ablation studies explicitly disabling or removing episodic/reflection memory components
Performance comparison between full hybrid agent and episodic-component-ablated variant
Complex or compositional task environments
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report an ablation experiment that disables the episodic/reflection memory component of a hybrid agent and compares performance to the full hybrid system on the same task.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Removing episodic memory leads to measurable performance degradation (lower success rate, score, or reward) compared to the full hybrid agent.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Ablating episodic memory has no significant negative effect, or improves performance compared to the full hybrid system.

rubric.specific_prediction: On long-horizon, compositional text game tasks (e.g., requiring navigation plus inventory management, or multi-stage goal completion), hybrid memory agents outperform agents using only unstructured episodic memory or reflection-only approaches.
operational_signals: Comparison with reflection-only, unstructured episodic, or summarization-only memory baselines
Tasks explicitly described as long-horizon, multi-step, or compositional
Performance metrics such as success rate, completion rate, or final score on hard task variants
rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate hybrid memory agents against unstructured episodic or reflection-only baselines on tasks explicitly characterized as long-horizon or compositional, reporting task success or performance metrics.
rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents substantially outperform unstructured episodic or reflection-only baselines on long-horizon or compositional tasks.
rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured episodic or reflection-only memory agents match or exceed hybrid memory agents' performance on long-horizon, compositional text game tasks.

theory_statement_law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_statement_law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory Yields Superior Generalization and Sample Efficiency
law.theory_statement: LLM-based agents in high-dimensional, partially observable, multi-step text games achieve substantially better generalization, faster convergence, and higher ultimate performance when equipped with hybrid memory architectures that combine structured semantic state representations (e.g., knowledge/belief graphs) with episodic/reflection-based memory modules for experience and procedural recall, as compared to agents relying on a single memory modality or naive in-context windowing.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based agents in interactive fiction (IF), text-adventure, multi-agent simulations, and real or synthetic multi-step text games featuring partial observability, complex environments, sparse/deferred rewards, compositional tasks (e.g., navigation, inventory management), and long planning horizons.
special_cases: In environments that are fully observable with trivial state/action structure, single-modality or no memory augmentation suffices.
Tasks requiring only short-term recall (≤ context window) see little or no benefit from hybridization.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne/AriGraph integrates knowledge graph and episodic memory; outperforming all baselines including those with full-history or reflection/episodic memory only. Ablations (disabling episodic or using full-history, RAG, iterative summarization, or Simulacra) always underperform.

GATA, KG-A2C, MoE Thespian and extension agents with knowledge graph consistently outperform non-structured baselines and text-only RL or episodic-only agents in benchmarks such as TextWorld, Jericho, LIGHT, and custom role-playing worlds.

Generative Agents (Smallville) and MemInsight separately demonstrate the necessity to combine different types of memory (e.g., long-term episodic memory with reflections/plans or attribute-augmented retrieval) for persisting social identity and reasoning fidelity.
MC-DML and RAP show that integrating both cross-trial/failure reflections and short-term trajectory memory yields the highest gains over baselines with only single-type memory.

Reflection-only (Reflexion), full-history, unstructured episodic, count-based, or summarization-only memory fail to match hybrid/structured performance and scalability, especially on long-horizon, multi-step, or compositional tasks (e.g., Cleaning, NetHack, Cooking Hard, Treasure Hunt Hard).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory integration has been implemented in some prior systems (e.g., AriGraph), discussed in cognitive science, and episodic/semantic separation in RL and memory-augmented models, but usually as design rationale or agent details—rarely as a formal requirement or general law.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law synthesizes and formalizes, at the algorithmic level, that hybridization (not mere addition) of structured/graph and episodic/reflection memory is a necessary condition for robust scalable performance in LLM text-game agents, based on exhaustive comparative evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Existing works support the principle in context, but this is the first systematic, general law stated as both necessary and sufficient for task class and performance regime.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [hybrid agent shows SOTA but not generalized as law]
Park et al. (2023) Generative Agents [episodic+reflection memory for social simulation, unstructured]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [structured augmentation for retrieval]
Wang et al. (2020) Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs [structured belief graph, not hybrid law]

predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured episodic or reflection-only memory agents match or exceed hybrid memory agents' performance on long-horizon, compositional text game tasks.

law.theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
law.theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory Yields Superior Generalization and Sample Efficiency
law.theory_statement: LLM-based agents in high-dimensional, partially observable, multi-step text games achieve substantially better generalization, faster convergence, and higher ultimate performance when equipped with hybrid memory architectures that combine structured semantic state representations (e.g., knowledge/belief graphs) with episodic/reflection-based memory modules for experience and procedural recall, as compared to agents relying on a single memory modality or naive in-context windowing.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based agents in interactive fiction (IF), text-adventure, multi-agent simulations, and real or synthetic multi-step text games featuring partial observability, complex environments, sparse/deferred rewards, compositional tasks (e.g., navigation, inventory management), and long planning horizons.
special_cases: In environments that are fully observable with trivial state/action structure, single-modality or no memory augmentation suffices.
Tasks requiring only short-term recall (≤ context window) see little or no benefit from hybridization.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne/AriGraph integrates knowledge graph and episodic memory; outperforming all baselines including those with full-history or reflection/episodic memory only. Ablations (disabling episodic or using full-history, RAG, iterative summarization, or Simulacra) always underperform.

GATA, KG-A2C, MoE Thespian and extension agents with knowledge graph consistently outperform non-structured baselines and text-only RL or episodic-only agents in benchmarks such as TextWorld, Jericho, LIGHT, and custom role-playing worlds.

Generative Agents (Smallville) and MemInsight separately demonstrate the necessity to combine different types of memory (e.g., long-term episodic memory with reflections/plans or attribute-augmented retrieval) for persisting social identity and reasoning fidelity.
MC-DML and RAP show that integrating both cross-trial/failure reflections and short-term trajectory memory yields the highest gains over baselines with only single-type memory.

Reflection-only (Reflexion), full-history, unstructured episodic, count-based, or summarization-only memory fail to match hybrid/structured performance and scalability, especially on long-horizon, multi-step, or compositional tasks (e.g., Cleaning, NetHack, Cooking Hard, Treasure Hunt Hard).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory integration has been implemented in some prior systems (e.g., AriGraph), discussed in cognitive science, and episodic/semantic separation in RL and memory-augmented models, but usually as design rationale or agent details—rarely as a formal requirement or general law.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law synthesizes and formalizes, at the algorithmic level, that hybridization (not mere addition) of structured/graph and episodic/reflection memory is a necessary condition for robust scalable performance in LLM text-game agents, based on exhaustive comparative evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Existing works support the principle in context, but this is the first systematic, general law stated as both necessary and sufficient for task class and performance regime.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [hybrid agent shows SOTA but not generalized as law]
Park et al. (2023) Generative Agents [episodic+reflection memory for social simulation, unstructured]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [structured augmentation for retrieval]
Wang et al. (2020) Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs [structured belief graph, not hybrid law]

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In complex, partially observable, multi-step text games, LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures (combining structured semantic representations such as knowledge/belief graphs with episodic/reflection-based memory) achieve higher task success rates and final scores than agents using only structured memory, only episodic memory, or naive in-context windowing alone.
operational_signals: Direct comparison of hybrid memory agents against single-modality baselines (structured-only, episodic-only, or context-window-only)
Task success rate, completion rate, score, or reward metrics in text game environments
Environments characterized as partially observable, multi-step, or compositional (e.g., TextWorld, Jericho, interactive fiction)
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report performance metrics (e.g., success rate, score, reward) for both a hybrid memory agent and at least one single-modality baseline (structured-only, episodic-only, or window-only) on the same text game task with partial observability and multi-step requirements.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents consistently outperform all single-modality baselines across multiple tasks or environments, especially on complex or long-horizon scenarios.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents (structured-only, episodic-only, or context-window-only) match or exceed hybrid memory agents' performance on complex, partially observable, multi-step text games.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In complex text games, LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures reach target performance thresholds in fewer episodes, steps, or interactions than agents using only a single memory modality.
operational_signals: Learning curves showing performance vs. number of episodes, trials, or environment steps
Comparative sample efficiency metrics (episodes to convergence, steps to threshold performance)
Direct comparison between hybrid and single-modality memory agents
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report learning dynamics (e.g., performance over time/episodes) for both hybrid and single-modality memory agents, demonstrating convergence speed or sample efficiency differences.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents reach performance milestones significantly faster (fewer episodes or steps) than single-modality baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents converge as fast as or faster than hybrid memory agents to equivalent performance levels.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: LLM agents with hybrid memory architectures demonstrate better zero-shot or few-shot generalization to new text game environments, unseen tasks, or harder variants compared to agents using only structured memory, only episodic memory, or naive context windowing.
operational_signals: Transfer or generalization experiments (training on one set of games/tasks, testing on new or harder variants)
Performance metrics on held-out environments or difficulty levels
Explicit comparison of hybrid vs. single-modality agents on out-of-distribution tasks
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate both hybrid and single-modality memory agents on at least one generalization scenario (new environment, unseen task type, or increased difficulty) distinct from training conditions.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents maintain higher performance or show smaller performance degradation on new/harder tasks compared to single-modality baselines.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Single-modality memory agents generalize as well as or better than hybrid memory agents to new environments or harder task variants.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In long-horizon text games, hybrid memory agents achieve better or equivalent performance compared to full-history retention agents while using fewer tokens or reduced memory footprint.
operational_signals: Comparison of hybrid memory vs. full-history or unlimited context baselines
Performance metrics (success rate, score) alongside memory or token usage statistics
Long-horizon or multi-episode task scenarios
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must compare hybrid memory agents against full-history baselines on long-horizon tasks, reporting both performance and resource metrics (e.g., tokens, memory size, context length).
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents match or exceed full-history performance while demonstrating lower token usage, memory footprint, or computational cost.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Full-history retention consistently outperforms hybrid memory agents on long-horizon tasks, or hybrid memory requires comparable or greater resource usage without performance gains.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: In text game tasks that require only short-term recall within the LLM's context window and have simple state/action structure, hybrid memory architectures show negligible performance improvement over naive in-context windowing or no memory augmentation.
operational_signals: Comparison of memory-augmented vs. baseline agents on explicitly short-horizon or simple tasks
Task descriptions indicating full observability, short episode length, or trivial state structure
Performance metrics showing small or no effect size
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate hybrid or augmented memory agents against minimal-memory baselines on tasks explicitly characterized as short-horizon, fully observable, or simple, reporting comparable performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Performance differences between hybrid memory and baseline agents are minimal (within error margins or statistically insignificant) on short-horizon or simple tasks.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Hybrid memory provides substantial performance gains even on tasks requiring only short-term recall within the context window and having trivial state structure.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: Removing the structured memory component (e.g., knowledge graph, belief graph) from a hybrid memory agent results in degraded performance on complex text games, with the agent performing worse than the full hybrid system.
operational_signals: Ablation studies explicitly disabling or removing structured memory components
Performance comparison between full hybrid agent and structured-component-ablated variant
Complex or compositional task environments
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report an ablation experiment that disables the structured memory component of a hybrid agent and compares performance to the full hybrid system on the same task.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Removing structured memory leads to measurable performance degradation (lower success rate, score, or reward) compared to the full hybrid agent.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Ablating structured memory has no significant negative effect, or improves performance compared to the full hybrid system.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: Removing the episodic or reflection-based memory component from a hybrid memory agent results in degraded performance on complex text games, with the agent performing worse than the full hybrid system.
operational_signals: Ablation studies explicitly disabling or removing episodic/reflection memory components
Performance comparison between full hybrid agent and episodic-component-ablated variant
Complex or compositional task environments
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must report an ablation experiment that disables the episodic/reflection memory component of a hybrid agent and compares performance to the full hybrid system on the same task.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Removing episodic memory leads to measurable performance degradation (lower success rate, score, or reward) compared to the full hybrid agent.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Ablating episodic memory has no significant negative effect, or improves performance compared to the full hybrid system.

predictive_rubric.specific_prediction: On long-horizon, compositional text game tasks (e.g., requiring navigation plus inventory management, or multi-stage goal completion), hybrid memory agents outperform agents using only unstructured episodic memory or reflection-only approaches.
operational_signals: Comparison with reflection-only, unstructured episodic, or summarization-only memory baselines
Tasks explicitly described as long-horizon, multi-step, or compositional
Performance metrics such as success rate, completion rate, or final score on hard task variants
predictive_rubric.strong_test_requirement: A paper must evaluate hybrid memory agents against unstructured episodic or reflection-only baselines on tasks explicitly characterized as long-horizon or compositional, reporting task success or performance metrics.
predictive_rubric.what_does_support_look_like: Hybrid memory agents substantially outperform unstructured episodic or reflection-only baselines on long-horizon or compositional tasks.
predictive_rubric.what_does_contradiction_look_like: Unstructured episodic or reflection-only memory agents match or exceed hybrid memory agents' performance on long-horizon, compositional text game tasks.

predictive_evaluation.evidence_quote_or_locator: Table 3 shows Full-context baselines compared to Hindsight, but the paper does not report token usage or memory footprint comparisons.

Tables 3 and 4 compare Hindsight to various memory systems on long-horizon conversational benchmarks, but do not explicitly isolate 'unstructured episodic' vs. 'hybrid structured-episodic' as the theory requires.
